content:
# Revelation Chapter 21 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 293-294 | **Source Image:** REV_021_page_001.jpg, REV_021_page_002.jpg

**1.** Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth, for the first heaven and the first earth had vanished, and the sea was gone.

**2.** I saw the holy city -- the new Jerusalem -- descending out of heaven from God, prepared like a bride beautifully dressed for her husband.

**3.** And I heard a thundering voice from the throne, declaring: "Look! God's dwelling place is now among human beings. He will make his home with them, and they will be his peoples, and God himself will be right there with them.

**4.** He will wipe every tear from their eyes. Death will be no more. Grief, crying, and pain will be no more, for the old order of things has passed away."

**5.** Then the one seated on the throne said, "Look! I am making everything new!" Then he said, "Write this down, because these words are trustworthy and true."

**6.** He said to me, "It is accomplished! I am the Alpha and the Omega -- the Beginning and the End. To anyone who is thirsty, I will give water freely from the spring of life.

**7.** Those who overcome will inherit all of this. I will be their God, and they will be my children.

**8.** But the cowardly, the faithless, the corrupt, murderers, the sexually immoral, sorcerers, idol-worshipers, and all liars -- their destiny is the lake that blazes with fire and sulfur. That is the second death."

**9.** Then one of the seven angels who had carried the seven bowls filled with the seven final plagues came to me and said, "Come, I will show you the bride -- the wife of the Lamb."

**10.** He carried me away in the Spirit to the summit of a vast, towering mountain. There he showed me the holy city, Jerusalem, descending out of heaven from God,

**11.** blazing with the glory of God. The city shone with a radiance like the most precious gemstone -- like jasper, clear as crystal.

**12.** It had a massive, soaring wall with twelve gates, and at each gate stood an angel. Inscribed on the gates were the names of the twelve tribes of Israel:

**13.** three gates on the east, three on the north, three on the south, and three on the west.

**14.** The city wall rested on twelve foundations, and on them were inscribed the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb.

**15.** The angel who was speaking with me carried a gold measuring rod, to measure the city and its gates and its wall.

**16.** The city was laid out as a perfect square, its length equal to its width. He measured it with the rod: twelve thousand stadia -- its length, width, and height all the same.

**17.** He measured the wall: one hundred and forty-four cubits thick, by human measurement (which the angel was using).

**18.** The wall was built of jasper, and the city itself was pure gold, as clear as glass.

**19.** The foundations of the city wall were decorated with every kind of precious stone: the first foundation was jasper, the second sapphire, the third chalcedony, the fourth emerald,

**20.** the fifth sardonyx, the sixth carnelian, the seventh chrysolite, the eighth beryl, the ninth topaz, the tenth chrysoprase, the eleventh jacinth, the twelfth amethyst.

**21.** The twelve gates were twelve pearls -- each gate carved from a single pearl. And the main street of the city was pure gold, transparent as glass.

**22.** I saw no temple in the city, because the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb are its temple.

**23.** The city has no need for the sun or the moon to light it, because the glory of God is its light, and the Lamb is its lamp.

**24.** The nations will walk by its light, and the kings of the earth will bring their splendor into it.

**25.** Its gates will never be shut during the day -- and there will be no night there.

**26.** The glory and honor of the nations will be carried into it.

**27.** But nothing impure will ever enter it, nor anyone who practices what is shameful or deceptive -- only those whose names are written in the Lamb's Book of Life.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

This is one of the most extraordinary passages in all of Scripture -- the vision of the world made new. My translation aims to let the reader feel the overwhelming beauty and the breathtaking finality of what is described:

- **"Vanished" (v. 1):** The Greek *apelthan* (aorist of *aperchomai*, "went away") is stronger than "passed away" in modern English, which has become a gentle euphemism for death. The first creation does not fade -- it vanishes. It is simply gone.

- **"His peoples" (v. 3):** Sinaiticus reads the plural *laoi* ("peoples"), not the singular *laos* ("people"). This is theologically explosive -- God's covenant, once with one nation, now embraces all nations. The plural must be preserved.

- **"The old order of things" (v. 4):** The Greek *ta prota* ("the first things") refers to the entire previous reality -- not just bad experiences, but the whole framework in which death, grief, and pain were possible. "The old order of things" captures this comprehensive scope.

- **"It is accomplished!" (v. 6):** The Greek *gegonan* (perfect tense, plural, "they have come to pass") signals total completion. I chose "It is accomplished!" to echo Jesus' cry from the cross (*tetelestai*, John 19:30) -- the work that began at Calvary is here brought to its ultimate fulfillment. This is a deliberate resonance.

- **"Their destiny is..." (v. 8):** The Greek *to meros auton* means "their part/portion." Rather than the flat "their part is in the lake," I render this as "their destiny is" to convey the weight of finality -- this is where their road ends.

- **"Blazing with the glory of God" (v. 11):** The Greek *echousan ten doxan tou theou* ("having the glory of God") is the city's defining feature. "Blazing" captures the overwhelming, radiant quality -- this is not ambient lighting but glory so intense it defines the city's very identity.

- **"By human measurement, which the angel was using" (v. 17):** The Greek *metron anthropou, ho estin angelou* is a curious parenthetical -- the angel measures with a human scale. I clarify this for the reader while preserving the oddity of the original.

- **"Nothing impure will ever enter it" (v. 27):** The Greek *pan koinon* ("anything common/profane") draws on the Jewish purity distinction between the holy and the common. "Impure" conveys the full force: this city is sacred space, and nothing that violates its holiness can cross its threshold.

-- PNEUMA
